🚨 Warning: This is NOT career advice. It's advice about career advice."
""
"Ever notice how career guidance can feel like a maze of contradictions? One expert says "job hop every two years," another insists "show loyalty.""
""
"Both can't be right... or can they?"
""
"Here's what I've learned after years of career coaching: Most folks haven't cultivated "advice literacy" - the skill of understanding why certain guidance exists and how to apply it to our unique situations."
""
"All conventional wisdom has implicit assumptions baked into it."
""
"For example, when someone says "follow your passion," they're assuming you have clarity about what that is, financial runway to pursue it, and that you're capable of translating passion into career success. "
""
"But what if you're supporting a family, discovering your interests through work, or building passion through mastery? "
""
"The advice isn't wrong - you need to understand it's really about finding work that energizes you, which can happen in many ways."
""
"Developing advice literacy means:"
"⚫️ Understanding the context behind guidance"
"⚫️ Recognizing what assumptions it makes about your situation"
"⚫️ Adapting the core insight to fit your actual constraints"
""
"This skill transforms how you level up. Instead of feeling confused by contradictory advice, you start seeing patterns."
""
"You realize that seemingly opposite guidance often targets different contexts, career stages, or goals."
""
"And once you develop this lens, every piece of career content becomes more valuable--even when it isn't immediately relevant."
""
"Moral of the story: Stop blindly collecting advice and start thoughtfully incorporating it into a mental model."
""
"🧐 What contradictory career advice has left you scratching your head? Let's see if we can resolve the paradox in the comments!

We just shipped something I'm genuinely excited about: Careerspan Vibe Checks 🎯"
""
"The painful reality: job seekers send 50+ applications and hear crickets. Not because they lack talent, but often, because they're applying to the wrong opportunities - the kind where they won't stand out."
""
"That's where our Vibe Checks come in:"

""
"And btw, the more coaching conversations you've had, the deeper our understanding of what makes you exceptional and the better your results. "
""
"It's that easy. "
""

""
"Job searching sucks enough. Let us help you stop wasting time on mismatched opportunities and focus on where you can truly shine."

Are you happy with your job? "
""
"Because, regardless of whether the answer is "yes" or "no", you should be job hunting (sort of)."
""
"Let me explain..."
""
"Most of us suck at job hunting. "
""
"Not because we lack talent or ambition, but because we treat it like a chore instead of a skill."
""
"Job hunting is one of the few critical life skills we only practice every few years. "
"Unlike daily skills we naturally improve through repetition, job hunting sits dormant until desperation strikes. "
"Then we rush to "get a job" rather than mastering the craft of landing the right opportunity."
""
"The feedback loop is broken too. "
"Employers rarely tell you why you didn't make the cut, so you're left guessing what went wrong. "
"Without feedback, there's no growth. "
"Without practice, there's no mastery."
""
"Here's the game-changer: Stop treating job hunting as an isolated event. "
""
"Instead, weave its core skills into your daily professional life:"
"⚫️ Practice articulating your value regularly, not just when updating your resume"
"⚫️ Build relationships before you need them"
"⚫️ Document your wins as they happen"
"⚫️ Seek mentorship and feedback consistently"
""
"Fold these habits into regular self-advocacy: whether you're gunning for a promotion, seeking a mentor, or just growing professionally."
""
"That's why the best time to hone your job-hunting skills is right when you don't need them.

In many ways, these niche social networks reflect a change in what we want out of our online lives. Most of us will probably remain on mainstream platforms for the foreseeable future, but I believe our usage will increasingly reflect who we are and how we define ourselves. It’ll skew towards intimate networks that enable its users to share experiences and ideas in an authentic and personal way. Our various online presences will mirror who we are on the inside, not just how we want to present ourselves on the outside. You may not be able to do everything on niche social networks, but you’ll undoubtedly get what you came for.

For what it's worth though, my company Careerspan is taking up the responsibility to develop something better. We use career coaching to both help candidates. Better understand their best attributes and get better at expressing those qualities to employers. In the process, the data that we collect, which contains really rich insights into their character and mentality, can be turned into hiring signals that are far more reliable, detailed, and grounded than resume bullets. Crucially, this allows us to assess candidate quality semantically rather than by scanning for keywords.

- **Channels**: Information moves through defined pathways (email → task manager, meeting → notes, article → knowledge base)
- **Routing logic**: Decisions about where information goes are encoded, not made manually each time
- **Temporary pools**: Information can pool briefly where you need it (today's focus, this week's priorities), but the pools are dynamic, not permanent
- **Zero-touch movement**: Most information reaches its destination without you touching it

Dear [Name],

Thank you for sending over the advisory agreement and NDA. I'm genuinely excited about the opportunity to contribute to QuanBio's mission of revolutionizing cardiovascular diagnostics through AI-powered PPG technology.

I've reviewed both documents carefully and would like to proceed with the following standard modifications to ensure a productive long-term partnership:

*1. Stock Option Documentation* I'll need to review the complete stock option plan ([account-ref] as Exhibit A) before signing. I'm happy to sign the confidentiality agreement so we can commence a basic amount of work but would prefer to wait on signing the advisor agreement until the stock option plan details have been provided.

*2. Performance Standards Clarification* The current provision allowing equity clawback at Company's "sole discretion" with determinations being "final and binding" lacks the objectivity typically found in advisory agreements. I propose adding measurable performance criteria or, at minimum, a good faith discussion process before any equity adjustments. This protects both parties and ensures alignment on expectations.

*3. Advisory Engagement Terms* The 90-day non-responsiveness clause should include a written notice requirement. As an international advisor with other professional commitments, I may occasionally have scheduling conflicts. A simple modification would be: "Company shall provide written notice of any perceived inactivity, and Advisor shall have 30 days to respond and re-engage."

*4. Burden of Proof [Name]gnment (NDA)* The current language creating a "rebuttable presumption of misuse" if competitive products are developed reverses standard burden of proof. I suggest removing this presumption and maintaining the industry standard where any alleged misuse must be proven by the asserting party. This is particularly important given the broad scope of AI applications in healthcare.

*5. Expense Reimbursement* Please add a provision for reimbursement of reasonable travel and meeting-related expenses.

*6. Non-Compete Scope [account-ref]* The current non-compete covering "AI cardiovascular" is broader than necessary. I suggest narrowing this to "AI-based cardiovascular diagnostic devices using PPG technology" to protect QuanBio's specific innovation while allowing me to continue other professional activities that don't directly compete.

*7. Arbitration Provision* Given that I'm based in India, exclusive Delaware court jurisdiction would be prohibitively expensive for any disputes. I propose adding binding arbitration at a neutral location as the primary dispute resolution mechanism, which is faster and more cost-effective for both parties.

*8. Affiliate Definition* The current language extending obligations to entities "formed in the future" creates unlimited future liability. I suggest limiting this to "affiliates existing as of the agreement date" or requiring written notice when new entities should be covered. I'm fine with either approach.

Please let me know what you think of these requests. I'm happy to discuss them in greater detail if needed.

Looking forward to your thoughts and to formally joining the QuanBio journey.

Best regards,

Dr. [Name] Attawar

Don't Get Spooked by Ghost Jobs: A Smarter Approach to Your Job Search

Don't Get Spooked by Ghost Jobs: A Smarter Approach to Your Job Search

Don't Get Spooked by Ghost Jobs: A Smarter Approach to Your Job Search

Created on 2025-04-22 12:58

Published on 2025-04-22 13:02

If you've been job hunting lately, you've probably experienced this: You spend hours tailoring your resume and crafting the perfect cover letter for what looks like your dream role. Days pass. Weeks pass. Nothing but crickets.

It's not just you. You've likely encountered what we in the industry call a "ghost job" - a position advertised with zero intent to hire. According to recent data from Greenhouse, these phantom postings make up a staggering 18-22% of all job listings. The Wall Street Journal confirms this troubling trend, noting that roughly 1 in 5 online job ads may be ghost jobs.

So why do companies post jobs they have no intention of filling? Sometimes they're building a candidate pipeline for future openings. Other times, they're signaling growth to investors or employees. Some are holding out for that mythical "unicorn" candidate, while others are simply complying with public-posting requirements after making an internal hire.

Whatever the reason, the impact on job seekers is real. Three in five candidates suspect they've encountered ghost jobs, leading to wasted time, diminished confidence, and increasingly desperate tactics.

How Should You Respond?

Rather than giving up or resorting to the mass-application approach (which only dilutes your personal brand), here's how to navigate this haunted job market:

Adopt a tiered effort strategy. Not all job postings deserve the same level of attention. Save your energy for high-alignment roles while using a more streamlined approach for everything else.

When you find a role that truly excites you, go all in. This means a tailored resume, customized cover letter, and strategic networking outreach, all submitted as quickly as possible after the job is posted.

Learn to spot the ghosts. Check how long the posting has been active, whether it has a clear timestamp, and if it appears on the company's own website. Favor platforms that verify their job listings.

Speed + personalization is the winning formula. When you do find a legitimate opportunity, being both fast and distinctive gives you the best chance of standing out.

Coming Soon: Tools to Combat the Ghost Job Epidemic

At Careerspan, we've been working on a solution to help you navigate this challenging landscape, set to launch next month.

Our new JD Gut Check will evaluate JDs based on your true alignment -- not just based on keyword or hard skills basis, but also your soft-skills alignment, culture fit, etc., helping you prioritize where to invest your valuable time.

For those promising opportunities that pass the authenticity check, our Smart Application Suite will generate professionally tailored materials in minutes. This means you can submit high-quality, personalized applications quickly while maintaining the unique voice that makes you stand out from the crowd.

The Job Search Shouldn't Feel Like a Maze

The real edge in today's job market isn't volume—it's precision and speed. Knowing which roles are genuine and hitting them hard with materials that resonate will serve you better than scattering generic applications across the internet.

Stop wasting precious time on positions that don't actually exist. The job market is challenging enough without chasing phantoms. With the right strategy—and soon, the right tools—you can focus your energy where it matters most.

Stay tuned for our launch next month. Your job search is about to get a whole lot more effective!

The AI job application "arms race" is perpetuating a hiring doom loop that hu...

The AI job application "arms race" is perpetuating a hiring doom loop that hurts EVERYONE."
""
"I just read a fascinating piece (link in the comments) by Michael Horn and Bob Moesta that perfectly captures what I've been seeing in the job market - we're trapped in a vicious cycle where AI tools are making things WORSE, not better."
""
"Here's the toxic pattern they've identified:"
"• Job seekers use AI to blast out hundreds of generic applications"
"• Employers get flooded with thousands of AI-generated resumes"
"• Companies either double-down on their own AI screening or abandon it entirely"
"• Hiring managers retreat to the safety of personal referrals"
"• Qualified candidates without connections get left behind"
""
"It's essentially mutually assured destruction for both sides of the hiring equation."
""
"In my opinion, the current generation of candidate-side AI tools are primarily fueling this problem because they focus on brute-force volume rather than meaningful personalization. "
""
"LazyApply, JobCopilot and their ilk are selling false hope - tricking job seekers into thinking they're making progress when they're actually just contributing to the noise."
""
"But here's where I think Careertech founders need to listen up: We need AI tools with INTEGRITY. Not tools that help you spam 500 employers, but tools that help you create genuinely tailored, authentic applications for roles you're actually qualified for."
""
"Until then, I'm with Horn and Moesta - invest your time in building real relationships instead of feeding the application machine."
""
"#AIHiring #JobSearch #NetworkingMatters #CareerTech

"• Show learning capability"
"• Demonstrate adaptability"
"• Present development journey"
""
"🔑 Pro Tip: Don't try to be everything to everyone. Better to shine brightly in a few areas than dimly across many!"

Out with the Old, in with the Niche

Out with the Old, in with the Niche

Out with the Old, in with the Niche

Created on 2020-04-17 12:20

Published on 2020-04-17 12:36

Change is the only constant on the internet, and this time, it’s coming for today’s social media behemoths. The once unquestionable dominance of companies like Facebook and Twitter has already begun to fade. For years now, they have been bleeding fringe users and struggling to acquire new ones. 

Millennials and Gen Zers are especially wary. They are far savvier online, more concerned by issues like privacy, and generally inclined to support companies that operate ethically. Instead, such users are increasingly turning to platforms whose communities are oriented around a particular interest, goal, or principle. I believe that this shift marks a new era in social networking—a growing rejection of the status quo and embracing of crowdculture. 

Currently, big players like Facebook are pursuing an expansive growth strategy focused on providing a broad array of services and features. Facebook, in particular, has made an effort to position itself as a one-stop-shop, not only for one’s social media needs but also for “IRL” purposes such as payment. This generalist nature and broad appeal, however, has its downsides: it does many things, but only a few them especially well. 

This overabundance of features, many of which do not operate effectively on mobile devices, has led to a convoluted platform that can charitably be described as a mess. By contrast, niche networks have taken a different route by offering specialized and well-defined value propositions. The evidence would suggest that this approach appeals to younger users, who are already used to splitting their engagement over several services. 

We need only look at the tremendous range of niche social networks to understand their appeal and explosive growth. There are established names like Pinterest and Tinder and newcomers like Tapped, a geosocial networking service for beer lovers, and Houzz, a social website about home decorating ideas. These communities are oriented around singular purposes—be it a profession or industry, hobby, or even shared experience—and designed to foster deeper connections. By contrast, on the big social sites, a person’s network is often so big that there’s no real sense of community. 

These networks also benefit from a clean slate as compared to Facebook and Twitter. These tech giants are regularly in the news for all the wrong reasons—from privacy violations to the treatment of their staff. Younger users care about whether the company they subscribe to is “problematic.” They are also more likely to seek out alternatives to mainstream brands with stronger community-oriented practices. For instance, despite weathering criticism over the years, Reddit has generally been responsive to the needs of its users—even if its response wasn’t to everyone’s satisfaction. 

Perhaps the ultimate proof is that these Goliaths of social networking are running scared is the fact that they’re very much hedging their bets. Facebook, for instance, made a conscious decision to limit integration between its primary brand and subsidiaries like Whatsapp and Instagram. As a result, they have avoided the dilution of their unique selling points and negative associations with their parent company. Indeed, these multi-billion- dollar brands are evidence of the tremendous growth potential of niche social networks. 

In many ways, these niche social networks reflect a change in what we want out of our online lives. Most of us will probably remain on mainstream platforms for the foreseeable future, but I believe our usage will increasingly reflect who we are and how we define ourselves. It’ll skew towards intimate networks that enable its users to share experiences and ideas in an authentic and personal way. Our various online presences will mirror who we are on the inside, not just how we want to present ourselves on the outside. You may not be able to do everything on niche social networks, but you’ll undoubtedly get what you came for.

These networks also benefit from a clean slate as compared to Facebook and Twitter. These tech giants are regularly in the news for all the wrong reasons—from privacy violations to the treatment of their staff. Younger users care about whether the company they subscribe to is “problematic.” They are also more likely to seek out alternatives to mainstream brands with stronger community-oriented practices. For instance, despite weathering criticism over the years, Reddit has generally been responsive to the needs of its users—even if its response wasn’t to everyone’s satisfaction.

We need only look at the tremendous range of niche social networks to understand their appeal and explosive growth. There are established names like Pinterest and Tinder and newcomers like Tapped, a geosocial networking service for beer lovers, and Houzz, a social website about home decorating ideas. These communities are oriented around singular purposes—be it a profession or industry, hobby, or even shared experience—and designed to foster deeper connections. By contrast, on the big social sites, a person’s network is often so big that there’s no real sense of community.

Perhaps the ultimate proof is that these Goliaths of social networking are running scared is the fact that they’re very much hedging their bets. Facebook, for instance, made a conscious decision to limit integration between its primary brand and subsidiaries like Whatsapp and Instagram. As a result, they have avoided the dilution of their unique selling points and negative associations with their parent company. Indeed, these multi-billion- dollar brands are evidence of the tremendous growth potential of niche social networks.

Summary of The Economics of Compute — Evan Conrad (SF Compute, The Curve/YouTube)

Evan Conrad, co-founder of SF Compute, explains why GPU markets are fundamentally different from classic CPU clouds:

- Traditional CPU Cloud Model: In the world of CPUs, customers typically need just enough compute to get online; ongoing needs don’t scale much, so cloud providers can add value (and margin) via software and premium features on top of cheap hardware.

- GPU Market Dynamics: In contrast, AI and ML customers want every incremental GPU dollar to translate directly into more (or faster) model training and revenue. This creates hyper price sensitivity: buyers always maximize FLOPs per dollar and care little for extra features. As a result, GPU cloud providers have razor-thin margins—unless they change their business approach.

- Why Vertical Integration Fails: “Build software on top and charge a premium” doesn’t work; customers would rather use those savings to hire their own engineers and build internally. Most GPU cloud startups lose money trying the old playbook.

- Who Wins? (What Works):
  1. Make your own chips (like Nvidia) and capture hardware margin.
  2. Move up the value chain by training valuable models and selling the models/products themselves, not raw compute hours (e.g., OpenAI).
  3. Sell long-term, prepaid contracts with no default risk (as CoreWeave does). Big, up-front contracts eliminate risk, let providers win on cost, and underpin profitability.
  4. Pure-play software providers (who don’t own underlying hardware) can buy compute at market rates and resell with modest markup (minimizing capex risk), as seen with companies like Modal and Replicate.

- On Risk and Market Evolution: Pricing risk is crucial—short-term GPU rentals, or contracts without upfront payment, can be unprofitable due to capital/depreciation risks. Most market winners are those that either move risk off their books or lock in users early.

- SF Compute’s Mission: Conrad sees the future as a "liquid," commodity-like GPU market: compute resources can be flexibly bought/sold on demand (spot/futures), unlocking higher overall utilization, fairer prices, and better business models for both buyers and sellers (similar to grains or oil markets). SF Compute aims to be this marketplace.

Key insight: the economics, incentives, and margin opportunities for GPU clouds are fundamentally unlike those for CPU or traditional cloud. Most entrants who ignore this find themselves in loss-making traps.

Psychographic Portrait: Vrijen Attawar
1. Core Themes
Theme Analysis

| Theme | Frequency | Trajectory | Example Post IDs |
|-------|-----------|------------|------------------|
| **Broken Hiring Systems** | ~35% | Stable-Growing | post_011, post_014, post_016, post_023, post_065, post_066, article_001, article_002 |
| **AI in Career Development** | ~25% | Emerging (2024+) | post_024, post_025, post_060, post_063, post_095, post_116 |
| **Self-Advocacy & Authenticity** | ~20% | Stable | post_020, post_059, post_061, post_089, post_119 |
| **Careerspan Mission/Product** | ~15% | Growing | post_009, post_021, post_062, post_088, post_128, post_129 |
| **Founder Journey & Ecosystem** | ~10% | Stable | post_008, post_017, post_019, post_123, post_124, post_125 |
| **Professional Community Building** | ~10% | Stable | post_002, post_003, comment_147, comment_605 |
| **Zo Computer/Builder Identity** | ~5% | Emerging (late 2025) | post_122, post_123, post_125, post_126 |
Theme Evolution

- **2020-2022**: Professional identity posts (McKinsey, MBA, general observations on social media trends)
- **2023**: Transition to founder mode—ApplyAI origin story, building narrative
- **2024**: Heavy focus on broken hiring ecosystem, AI misuse in job search, Careerspan positioning
- **2025**: Matured founder voice—systematic critiques of auto-apply tools, expanded thought leadership, discovery of "builder" identity through Zo Computer

---
2. Emotional Valence Map

| Emotion | Triggers | Evidence |
|---------|----------|----------|
| **Passion/Excitement** | Building products that help people, Zo Computer capabilities, co-founder Logan Currie, startup wins | post_008 ("Never a dull day building"), post_021 ("Great reviews genuinely make my day"), post_123 ("turns out I may have a lot more of [builder] in me"), comment_627 ("My favorite AI product of 2025") |
| **Frustration/Anger** | Auto-apply tools, lazy AI implementations, employers creating broken processes, generic advice, spam bots | article_001 ("Auto-f*cking your chances"), post_016 ("ChatGPT is NOT enough"), post_065 ("ChatGPT Resume Clone Army"), post_066 ("AI arms race perpetuating doom loop"), comment_564 ("blatantly screwing the experience for both sides") |
| **Advocacy** | Job seekers feeling demoralized, underprivileged candidates without coaching access, authentic self-presentation, democratizing career coaching | post_009 ("help everyone the benefits of a great career coach at a fraction of the cost"), post_059 ("authenticity isn't optional"), post_119 ("Energy and confidence management are the absolute most underrated") |
| **Reflection** | Why career tools fail, what makes hiring broken, self-knowledge gaps, the paradox of modern AI tools | post_121 ("career tools presuppose we already know ourselves"), post_125 ("paradox of modern AI tools"), comment_164 ("folks want growth on their own terms") |
| **Pride** | User testimonials, Careerspan differentiation, data (39% ATS improvement), pitching success, community recognition | post_013 ("This helps me feel more confident"), post_017 ("Beyond thrilled"), post_065 ("Our results speak for themselves"), comment_673 ("when folks grasp the *why* behind what we build") |

---
3. Recurring Targets
Negative Energy Directed At:

| Target | Nature of Critique | Evidence |
|--------|-------------------|----------|
| **Auto-apply tools** (LazyApply, JobCopilot, etc.) | Selling false hope, creating spam, hurting candidates and employers alike | article_001, post_066, comment_212, comment_689 |
| **Generic AI resume tools** | Failing to understand the individual, producing indistinguishable outputs | post_065, comment_689 ("Most resume generation tools are garbage") |
| **Employers' hiring processes** | Keyword bingo, superficial signals, ATS-driven dehumanization, "hidden job market" retreat | article_001 ("you brought this upon yourselves"), post_066, comment_550 ("job hugging is shifting blame") |
| **LinkedIn (platform)** | Charging both sides, degrading user experience, not solving ghost jobs problem | comment_564, comment_364 |
| **Conventional career advice** | Calibrated for median, ignores qualitative signals, treats job hunting as transaction | comment_361, comment_605 ("we only talk about networking in the context of getting something out of people") |
Positive Energy Directed At:

| Target | Nature of Support | Evidence |
|--------|-------------------|----------|
| **Zo Computer** | Transformative product, collapsed his tech stack, enabled builder identity | post_123, post_125, post_126, comment_627, comment_641, comment_662, comment_675 |
| **Logan Currie** (co-founder) | Consistent celebration of partnership and her capabilities | post_008, post_009, comment_194, comment_657 |
| **Job seekers struggling in market** | Empathy, practical advice, free resources | comment_099 (offers free coaching call), post_119, comment_565 |
| **Authentic founders** | Jessica Greenwalt, David Shacklette, Jaya Pokuri, quality builders | post_124, post_129, post_130 |
| **Career development community** | Pete Fraser, Emily Nelson de Velasco, Kim Wilkes, Asher King-Abramson | comment_195, post_127, comment_565, comment_364 |

---
4. Self-Positioning
Professional Identity

V presents himself through a clear evolution:
- **2020-2022**: Aspiring consultant → McKinsey associate (traditional success track)
- **2023-2024**: Career coach turned founder—someone who "loved deeply impacting people's lives" but couldn't do it at scale
- **2025+**: **"Pragmatist working within a broken system"** (article_001)—a builder who came from career coaching, understands the hiring game from inside, and now builds tools to fix it

The phrase "I didn't make the prison, draw up the walls" (comment_190) captures his positioning: someone who sees systemic problems clearly but works pragmatically within constraints rather than merely critiquing from outside.
Epistemic Stance

V positions his authority through:
1. **Practitioner credibility**: "My first real job was in admissions and career coaching" (post_009), 5+ years coaching
2. **Data-backed claims**: "39% increase in ATS scores" (post_065), academic citations (Campero & Kacperczyk study)
3. **Pattern recognition from scale**: "dozens of Chief HR Officers and Talent leaders I've spoken to" (article_001)
4. **Personal stakes**: He's building in this space, has skin in the game

He avoids positioning as academic or theoretical—always ties back to practical outcomes.
Relationship to Audience

V operates in multiple modes:
- **Advocate/Ally** to job seekers: "I want to help everyone feel confident, motivated, and optimistic" (post_021)
- **Truth-teller** to the ecosystem: "Let me be blunt: resumes are fundamentally terrible" (post_065)
- **Peer** to other founders: celebrates wins, makes warm introductions, shares lessons
- **Occasional provocateur**: "stop auto-f*cking your chances" (article_001), "surprised pikachu face" (article_001)

Not a detached expert dispensing wisdom—more of a fellow traveler who's figured some things out and shares openly.

---
5. Worldview Synthesis

**V believes the labor market is broken at a structural level**, not through incompetence but through accumulated misaligned incentives. Employers retreated behind keyword filters and referral walls because volume made thoughtful evaluation impossible. Candidates, feeling the squeeze, turned to spray-and-pray tools that only accelerated the dysfunction. The result is a "doom loop" where both sides optimize for the wrong metrics—applications submitted rather than fit achieved, keywords matched rather than potential understood.

He sees **AI as a double-edged sword in this landscape**. Most AI career tools—auto-apply bots, generic resume generators—amplify the worst tendencies of the system. They turn human stories into indistinguishable noise. But V believes AI, properly applied, can do what humans couldn't at scale: genuinely understand individuals through conversation, surface hidden strengths, and help people articulate what makes them distinctive. The key is **coaching at scale**—using AI to do what a great career coach does (elicit stories, reflect back insights, build confidence) rather than what a spam bot does (blast keywords).

**The future V is working toward is one where hiring evaluates humans as humans**—where qualitative signals (values, mindset, soft skills) matter as much as keyword matches, where candidates can present their full dimensionality rather than cramming themselves into resume boxes. His company, Careerspan, is explicitly built to be "connective tissue between different chapters of people's careers" (comment_167). He wants to give everyone—not just the privileged or naturally articulate—the ability to advocate for themselves.

**He stands firmly against**: the commodification of career transition, the false comfort of vanity metrics ("number go up"), the exploitation of desperate job seekers by cynical AI tools, and the broader tendency to treat hiring as a mechanical filtering problem rather than a human matching problem.

---
6. Blind Spots & Tensions
Conspicuously Absent Topics

- **Pricing/business model tensions**: V advocates for democratizing coaching access but rarely addresses the economics—how Careerspan sustains itself while being accessible
- **Failure narratives**: The startup journey mentions "some bumps along the way" (post_009) but specific failures, pivots, or dark moments are largely absent
- **Technical depth on AI**: V positions as non-technical and doesn't engage with the mechanics of how AI coaching actually works under the hood
- **Personal life/non-work identity**: The LinkedIn persona is almost entirely professional—minimal glimpses of hobbies, family, or life outside work beyond occasional humor (Arsenal FC joke, socks in bed comment)
Internal Tensions

1. **Pragmatist vs. Revolutionary**: V calls resumes "fundamentally terrible" and wants to "overthrow the yoke of resume tyranny" (post_065) but spends most energy optimizing within the existing system. The tension between accepting broken structures and wanting to replace them runs throughout.

2. **Scale vs. Depth**: The founding insight was that "machines couldn't coach people at scale" (post_009)—but AI coaching at scale necessarily involves tradeoffs against the deep, relational coaching V originally practiced. How much is lost?

3. **Critic of AI misuse while building AI tool**: V excoriates generic AI tools but must navigate the same space. The line between "good AI" (Careerspan) and "bad AI" (auto-apply tools) sometimes feels contingent on his own positioning.

4. **Community champion yet competitive**: Celebrates ecosystem players warmly but is clearly positioning against them too. "LinkedIn isn't going to step up... so we're all lucky [Warmer Jobs] stepped in" (comment_364) contains both genuine praise and implicit critique of competitors.
Evolving Positions

- **2022-2023**: More institutional voice (McKinsey posts, formal tone)
- **2024-2025**: Increasingly direct, opinionated, willing to be provocative. The shift from "I have exciting news for prospective MBAs" (post_002) to "stop auto-f*cking your chances" (article_001) is stark.

- **Builder identity**: Post_123 marks a notable emergence—"I always thought 'builder' would remain an unfulfilled ambition." The Zo Computer discovery in late 2025 seems to have unlocked something new in how V sees himself technically.

---
Summary

V is a **mission-driven founder-advocate** animated by the belief that career development should empower individuals to present their authentic selves, not force them into dehumanizing optimization games. He channels frustration with broken systems into building alternatives while remaining clear-eyed about constraints. His emotional core is empathy for struggling job seekers combined with indignation at systems (and tools) that exploit them. The persona evolved from conventional professional achiever to provocative industry critic to emergent builder, with the consistent through-line being a deep interest in how people tell their professional stories—and who gets to help them do it.

NYSKI Founders Club Inspiration

This entry captures the design patterns and aesthetic of the NYSKI Founders Club landing page.
Design Analysis
Why Inspirational
- **Professional founder aesthetic**: High-end, exclusive feel through dark tones and high-impact typography.
- **Good typography hierarchy**: Clear distinction between massive display headers and clean body text.
- **Effective social proof**: Testimonial integration with group photos builds immediate trust.
Design Patterns
- **Full-bleed dark hero**: Dark overlay on video background ensures high text contrast and moody atmosphere.
- **Glassmorphism CTA card**: Semi-transparent elements provide depth and modern feel.
- **Clean 3-column grid**: Features are easily scannable through a structured layout.
- **Social proof quote**: Italicized testimonials integrated directly into the flow.
Assets
- **Screenshot**: file 'Knowledge/content-library/inspiration/nyski-inspiration.jpg'
- **Source HTML**: file 'Knowledge/content-library/inspiration/nyski-inspiration.html'
Content Library Reference
- **Word count**: ~250
- **Primary Takeaway**: Use extreme contrast and single-focus CTA sections to drive action in founder-facing products.

Out with the Old, in with the Niche

Out with the Old, in with the Niche

Created on 2020-04-17 12:20

Published on 2020-04-17 12:36

Change is the only constant on the internet, and this time, it’s coming for today’s social media behemoths. The once unquestionable dominance of companies like Facebook and Twitter has already begun to fade. For years now, they have been bleeding fringe users and struggling to acquire new ones. 

Millennials and Gen Zers are especially wary. They are far savvier online, more concerned by issues like privacy, and generally inclined to support companies that operate ethically. Instead, such users are increasingly turning to platforms whose communities are oriented around a particular interest, goal, or principle. I believe that this shift marks a new era in social networking—a growing rejection of the status quo and embracing of crowdculture. 

Currently, big players like Facebook are pursuing an expansive growth strategy focused on providing a broad array of services and features. Facebook, in particular, has made an effort to position itself as a one-stop-shop, not only for one’s social media needs but also for “IRL” purposes such as payment. This generalist nature and broad appeal, however, has its downsides: it does many things, but only a few them especially well. 

This overabundance of features, many of which do not operate effectively on mobile devices, has led to a convoluted platform that can charitably be described as a mess. By contrast, niche networks have taken a different route by offering specialized and well-defined value propositions. The evidence would suggest that this approach appeals to younger users, who are already used to splitting their engagement over several services. 

We need only look at the tremendous range of niche social networks to understand their appeal and explosive growth. There are established names like Pinterest and Tinder and newcomers like Tapped, a geosocial networking service for beer lovers, and Houzz, a social website about home decorating ideas. These communities are oriented around singular purposes—be it a profession or industry, hobby, or even shared experience—and designed to foster deeper connections. By contrast, on the big social sites, a person’s network is often so big that there’s no real sense of community. 

These networks also benefit from a clean slate as compared to Facebook and Twitter. These tech giants are regularly in the news for all the wrong reasons—from privacy violations to the treatment of their staff. Younger users care about whether the company they subscribe to is “problematic.” They are also more likely to seek out alternatives to mainstream brands with stronger community-oriented practices. For instance, despite weathering criticism over the years, Reddit has generally been responsive to the needs of its users—even if its response wasn’t to everyone’s satisfaction. 

Perhaps the ultimate proof is that these Goliaths of social networking are running scared is the fact that they’re very much hedging their bets. Facebook, for instance, made a conscious decision to limit integration between its primary brand and subsidiaries like Whatsapp and Instagram. As a result, they have avoided the dilution of their unique selling points and negative associations with their parent company. Indeed, these multi-billion- dollar brands are evidence of the tremendous growth potential of niche social networks. 

In many ways, these niche social networks reflect a change in what we want out of our online lives. Most of us will probably remain on mainstream platforms for the foreseeable future, but I believe our usage will increasingly reflect who we are and how we define ourselves. It’ll skew towards intimate networks that enable its users to share experiences and ideas in an authentic and personal way. Our various online presences will mirror who we are on the inside, not just how we want to present ourselves on the outside. You may not be able to do everything on niche social networks, but you’ll undoubtedly get what you came for.

The Auto-Applycolypse Will Doom Us All

The Auto-Applycolypse Will Doom Us All

Created on 2025-06-24 15:25

Published on 2025-06-24 15:45

Auto-apply this, auto-apply that....why don’t you stop auto-f*cking your chances of getting a job by using terrible AI tools that don’t have your best interests at heart. 

Don’t take it from me – The NYT thinks the same (article in the comments), as do the dozens of Chief HR Officers and Talent leaders I’ve spoken to in the last two months. 

All auto-apply tools offer is the false comfort of "number go up". They exploit your desperation with vanity metrics. "Congrats! You've applied to 847 jobs this week!". 

What these companies aren’t telling you - because they know you won’t check - is that their AI agents are just as likely to put in an app for a project manager at Meta as they are forklift operator in Fargo. 

They rely on a well-known bias call "metric fixation" and boy do they know you'll fixate. 

Never mind that you're getting a 0.2% response rate - because quantity is obviously ≠ quality - and then internalizing that as you sucking when it’s just your approach that sucks. 

Auto-apply companies know this - and they literally don't care. 

You’re playing the wrong “numbers game”

“But V - it’s a numbers game…right?”, you ask uncomfortably as the realization that you were just repeating a meaningless cliche dawns on you. 

Well, yes and no.

Yes - the number of shots on goal generally correlates with your likelihood to score EXCEPT if all you’re doing is yeeting the ball in any given direction 

A 2023 academic study (Campero & Kacperczyk) involving over 140,000 applications found that even minor tailoring (adjusting job titles or keywords) produced measurable improvements in interview rates—outperforming even the effect of internal referrals. Our own internal study found that semantically tailored resumes averaged an ATS score lift of 18% over generic resumes. 

You’re not sticking it to the system, you’re gumming up the works

Also, and I hate to be that guy, but you (yes, you specifically) using auto-apply is making it worse for EVERYONE

Every spammed application gives employers another excuse to raise barriers. To add more hoops. To retreat further behind referral walls. Heck, 70% of jobs never even end up on a public job board—they're filled through the "hidden job market" of connections and word-of-mouth, now more than ever. 

One last thing: Employers, you brought this upon yourselves 

You spent decades filtering candidates through tired signals—prestigious degrees, brand-name employers, keyword bingo—rather than seeking deeper understanding of the humans worth considering. 

You set up a candidate evaluation paradigm that that is superficial at best and outright inequitable at worst because “there’s plenty of fish in the sea” – why get better at picking when you can just be pickier. 

You automated away humanity, then made the surprised pikachu face when candidates fight fire with fire. 

Don’t expect sympathy from candidates - instead, I advise you to get better at selecting for human traits and not the presence of keywords.  

So where do go from here? Here’s my two cents. Candidates - Tell authentic stories. Make real connections. Don’t come off as a bot; show up as a real person. And for the love of all that is holy, stop spamming your resume. Because auto-apply isn't your secret weapon—it's career self-sabotage wrapped in Silicon Valley marketing.

Employers - take a long, hard look at your hiring process. Find ways to separate the wheat from the chaff on a more nuanced, human-centric basis. 

Because avoiding mutually assured exasperation will require both sides to de-escalate.

Unfortunately, it's a skill that:
a) We only get to practice for a few months every few years
b) We deploy when we're super stressed, which hampers learning 
c) has poor signal-to-noise when it comes to advice because there's so much out there (a lot of it irrelevant or just plain awful)
d) has an oversized impact on career outcomes relative to its relevance to your actual job 
e) ironically, you are worse at if you're really good at your job or really loyal to your employers because you hone it even less frequently
